retrieved_docs.title: Lipoprotein Lipase Deficiency -- Treatment / Management -- Novel Treatments
retrieved_docs.content: Several studies have demonstrated the benefits of nutraceuticals, including artichoke leaf extract, berberine, and bergamot. In a study involving over 700 patients, artichoke leaf extract resulted in a significant decrease in triglyceride levels, which is believed to be due to its effects on multiple liver pathways. [47]

retrieved_docs.title: Obesity Supplements -- Function -- Fucoxanthin
retrieved_docs.content: Garcinia Cambogia

retrieved_docs.title: Obesity Supplements -- Function -- Guar Gum
retrieved_docs.content: Irvingia gabonensis , also known as African mango, is a fruit native to Africa and commonly consumed in African cuisine. [55] The plant has many advantageous properties, including high fiber content and anti-diabetic and anti-lipidemic effects. Several studies have examined different formulations of I. gabonensis on weight and found beneficial results. A 2013 systematic review of the efficacy of I. gabonensis for weight management reported significant reductions in body weight, waist circumference, and total cholesterol. [56] An article published in 2018 studied the effect of 150 mg (milligrams) of I. gabonensis taken twice a day over 90 days found improved and statistically significant differences in waist circumference, serum glucose, and triglycerides. [57] While multiple studies have reported beneficial results on metabolic disease markers, small sample sizes, and mixed methods make it difficult to extrapolate a true clinical benefit. Overall, I. gabonensis appears to be safe and well-tolerated. The most common side effects include headache, flatulence, and difficulty sleeping. [58] [59] Further studies are needed to determine efficacy, dosing, and safety.

retrieved_docs.title: Obesity Supplements -- Function -- Fucoxanthin
retrieved_docs.content: Garcinia cambogia is a fruit-bearing tree native to Southeast Asia and India. The plant’s active compound, hydroxy citric acid (HCA), has several potential mechanisms for mediating weight loss. HCA is thought to decrease fatty acid synthesis and lipogenesis, suppress appetite by increasing serotonin availability in the brain, and increase hepatic glycogen synthesis, thus increasing satiety. In a 2011 meta-analysis, HCA decreased weight by 0.88 kg on average compared to a placebo. [38] While this finding did reach statistical significance, its clinical significance is questionable. Subsequent randomized controlled trials have failed to find a benefit. [39] [40] Furthermore, safety has been questioned due to reports of associated liver toxicity. While most of these cases occurred in patients taking combination products, 3 have occurred with Garcinia cambogia alone, and patients with liver disease should be cautioned. [41] [42]

retrieved_docs.title: Obesity Supplements -- Function -- Fucoxanthin
retrieved_docs.content: Fucoxanthin is a carotenoid found in brown seaweed and other algae. Preclinical data suggest that fucoxanthin might effectively treat obesity through its anti-lipidemic properties. Only 2 clinical trials have been conducted using fucoxanthin. In a 16-week double-blind placebo-controlled trial, 151 non-diabetic, obese, pre-menopausal women with and without non-alcoholic fatty liver disease were given a combination of fucoxanthin with pomegranate seed oil (PSO) at different doses (300 mg algae containing 2.4 mg fucoxanthin plus 300 mg PSO versus 200 mg algae containing 1.6 mg fucoxanthin plus 200 mg PSO). [36] The group taking the higher dose of combined fucoxanthin plus PSO had a statistically significant reduction in body weight, waist circumference, and other metabolic parameters. In a double-blind, placebo-controlled trial of 31 obese participants receiving 0.8 mg fucoxanthin plus 100 mg of PSO versus placebo for 16 weeks, no significant differences in body fat or weight loss were observed. Toxicological assessments of the combined fucoxanthin and PSO extract administered to rats found no teratogenic or toxic effects. [37] Of note, a statistically significant decrease in body weight and food intake occurred in the treated rats. Based on the results of the published data, no recommendations can be made about the efficacy of fucoxanthin for overweight and obesity treatment in humans, but preclinical and clinical data show promise.

retrieved_docs.title: Obesity Supplements -- Function -- Guar Gum
retrieved_docs.content: Hoodia gordonii is a succulent plant native to Africa. It contains a steroidal glycoside that acts centrally on the hypothalamus to suppress appetite. [53] While this popular supplement has been used for weight loss, the 1 randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled study examining H. gordonii found no significant improvement in energy intake, body weight, or fat percentage compared with a placebo. [54] Furthermore, adverse events were more common among those receiving H. gordonii , with altered skin sensation, headache, dizziness, and nausea among the most commonly reported symptoms. [54] Irvingia Gabonensis

retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Evaluate data on the efficacy of various dietary weight-loss supplements. Assess the mechanisms of action for dietary weight-loss supplements. retrieved_docs.title: Nonalcoholic Steatohepatitis (NASH) -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: The mainstay of treatment is lifestyle modification with the goal to achieve at least 5% to 10% of weight loss. Both biochemical and histological improvement was seen in patients who had weight loss, which is accomplished via calorie reduction combined with exercise. One to two cups of caffeinated drip coffee may be considered as part of the lifestyle modification measure, given the reported association of caffeinated drip coffee with decreased liver fibrosis and decreased incidence of hepatocellular carcinoma. Studies have shown that eliminating high fructose corn syrup and increasing omega three fats in the diet is beneficial. Thiazolidinediones appear to be beneficial in improving adipocyte insulin sensitivity and preventing inappropriate lipolysis. Their side effect profile (weight gain, congestive heart failure exacerbation, and osteoporosis), however, limits their use. Antioxidants such as vitamin E are of modest benefit and likely safe in non-diabetic patients; a dose of 800 units per day may be offered in such patients after explaining the risks of increased all-cause mortality with high dose vitamin E therapy. In patients with diabetes, we do not recommend giving a dose higher than 400 units per day. Bariatric surgery is an effective treatment and may be considered in patients with morbid obesity who would otherwise meet the criteria for bariatric surgery. Statins should not be withheld in patients with NAFLD due to pre-existing abnormal liver biochemical tests. Liver transplantation is the last resort in patients with decompensated cirrhosis, with the caveat that NAFLD can recur after transplant. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Nonalcoholic Steatohepatitis (NASH) -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Nonalcoholic, fatty liver disease (NAFLD) is defined as the ectopic accumulation of fat in the liver (hepatic steatosis) when no other causes of secondary liver fat accumulation are present. As minor deposition of fat can occur in healthy adults, deposition of fat in at least 5% of hepatocytes is considered pathologic. This activity will review the evaluation and management of NAFLD and the role of the health care team in managing patients with this condition.

retrieved_docs.content: Treatment of steatorrhea depends on the underlying conditions which cause steatorrhea.

retrieved_docs.title: Euphorbia tirucalli Toxicity -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Euphorbia tirucalli ingestions usually only cause mild symptoms, and treatment is supportive. [1] Cold fluids, ice, or ice cream may alleviate irritated oral mucosa, and viscous lidocaine may be considered for more severe cases. It is uncertain whether activated charcoal will help neutralize or prevent absorption of Euphorbia sap. However, it may be reasonable to administer in high plant ingestion cases that present within 1 hour, as long as the patient is otherwise an appropriate candidate for activated charcoal administration. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Obesity Supplements -- Function -- Chromium Picolinate
retrieved_docs.content: Citrus aurantium , or bitter orange, is derived from a fruit-bearing tree indigenous to Southeast Asia. Due to its sympathomimetic properties, bitter orange gained popularity as a weight-loss supplement after ephedra was banned because of adverse cardiovascular effects. [19] Like ephedra, Citrus aurantium has alpha and beta-adrenergic effects and is purported to increase metabolic rate and lipolysis. [20] Several studies have examined multi-ingredient products containing bitter orange. They have found these products to benefit weight loss, but the individual effect of bitter orange itself is difficult to ascertain. [21] When studied alone, bitter orange increases basal metabolic rate in adults. [22] A review of published and unpublished studies concluded that bitter orange extract and p-synephrine increase metabolism and energy expenditure, but there is a need for long-term efficacy studies in humans. [23]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 1453
retrieved_docs.content: plant seeds and flowers). These ancillary ingredients are likely intended to supply nutritional support, antioxidant capacity, and additional pharmacologic actions that complement the primary adrenergic and corticotropic effects. The repeated pattern of pairing epinephrine with corticotropin, together with other endocrine hormones, neurotransmitters, minerals, vitamins, and herbal components, suggests a therapeutic strategy aimed at modulating sympathetic activity, adrenal cortical output, thyroid function, and overall metabolic homeostasis. The inclusion of both synthetic and natural substances across the products underscores a multimodal approach to patient (or animal) care that leverages synergistic actions of catecholamines, ACTH, and supportive nutrients to address conditions such as allergic reactions, neuro‑behavioral imbalances, weight management, and systemic endocrine deficiencies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2317
retrieved_docs.content: Liver therapy drugs are identified as liver therapy agents and lipotropics, and the compound citiolone is a member of this group of liver therapy drugs.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1977
retrieved_docs.content: Klofibrát produces a physiologic effect of decreased lipolysis, thereby influencing lipid metabolism, but it is contraindicated in patients with تَشَمُّع كَبِدي (hepatic steatosis). Consequently, clinicians should consider the lipid‑lowering benefit of Klofibrát while avoiding its use in individuals with hepatic steatosis to prevent potential liver‑related complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1400
retrieved_docs.content: BILE AND LIVER THERAPY DRUGS represent a class of medications that function as bile therapy drugs and are simultaneously categorized under liver therapy and lipotropics. By acting as bile therapy agents, these drugs help promote bile production and flow, supporting digestive processes and hepatic clearance. Their classification as liver therapy agents reflects their broader role in enhancing overall liver function, protecting hepatocytes, and aiding recovery from liver injury. As lipotropic agents, they also facilitate the metabolism and mobilization of lipids, contributing to the regulation of fat processing in the body. The dual categorization underscores the overlapping therapeutic objectives of improving bile dynamics, supporting liver health, and optimizing lipid metabolism.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 155

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2063
retrieved_docs.content: Peripherally acting antiobesity products are classified as a specific type of antiobesity preparations, excluding diet products. This subclass of antiobesity agents exerts its therapeutic effects through peripheral mechanisms rather than central pathways, distinguishing them from diet products and centrally acting weight‑loss medications. Recognizing that peripherally acting antiobesity products belong to the broader category of antiobesity preparations, but are not considered diet products, helps clinicians select appropriate pharmacologic options for obesity management based on mechanism of action and product classification.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2467
retrieved_docs.content: Clofibrate (klofrolát) exerts a physiologic effect by decreasing lipolysis, which contributes to its lipid‑lowering action. Because of its pharmacologic profile, clofibrate is contraindicated in patients with renal failure (قصور الكلى), and should be avoided in this population to prevent adverse outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2216
retrieved_docs.content: Medium chain triglycerides are contraindicated in patients who have ارتفاع نسبة الدهون الثلاثية في الدم (elevated blood triglyceride levels), indicating that the use of medium chain triglycerides should be avoided in individuals with this condition because it may exacerbate or is not recommended for managing high triglyceride concentrations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1978
retrieved_docs.content: Bezafibrate is classified as a member of the fibrates, a group of plain lipid‑modifying drugs. As part of this drug class, bezafibrate shares the typical pharmacologic actions of fibrates, which are used to alter lipid profiles by lowering triglyceride levels and modestly increasing high‑density lipoprotein cholesterol.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2568
retrieved_docs.content: Selenium supplement therapy is employed with a therapeutic intent, indicating that its primary purpose is to provide a health‑benefiting effect.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 1375
retrieved_docs.content: Fat emulsion and glucosamine are both classified as dietary supplements, indicating that each is used to augment nutrition or support specific health needs. As dietary supplements, they are intended to be taken in addition to regular food intake to provide additional nutrients or functional compounds.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 2063
retrieved_docs.content: Glucagon-like peptide‑1 (GLP‑1) analogues constitute a class of therapeutic agents that mimic the incretin hormone GLP‑1, enhancing glucose‑dependent insulin secretion, inhibiting glucagon release, slowing gastric emptying, and promoting satiety. Liraglutide is a member of this GLP‑1 analogue class, and its pharmacologic actions make it valuable for the treatment of type 2 diabetes mellitus and for weight management in patients with obesity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 5 -- Community 2957
retrieved_docs.content: Diet therapy is recognized as an allowed qualifier for managing نوبة (seizure) and عضال غدي (glandular disease), indicating that dietary interventions are considered appropriate therapeutic options for these conditions.
